learning_objectives:
Apply Object-Oriented Programming
(OOP) principles to solve real-world problems

Understand how to architect a
multi-class system in C++

Manage data collections using STL containers like
std::vector

Implement business logic such as banking transactions and library
inventory tracking
Practice clean code habits and modular design

introduction:
Phase 15 is where
your theoretical knowledge transforms into practical skill. Instead of writing
isolated functions, you will build complete, interactive systems. We will focus
on designing software using classes that interact with each other, simulating
real-world scenarios like banking, education, and inventory management. This
phase emphasizes 'thinking in objects'—identifying the entities, their
properties, and their behaviors.

concept_explanation.what_is_it:
Project-based learning involves integrating multiple C++ concepts (OOP, STL,
File Handling) into a single cohesive application.
concept_explanation.why_do_we_need_it:
In
the professional world, software isn't just one class; it's a network of
interacting components. Projects teach you how to manage complexity, handle user
input effectively, and maintain data integrity.
concept_explanation.how_does_it_work:
We
identify 'Entities' (like a BankAccount or a Book), define their data (balance,
title), and implement 'Services' (deposit, borrow) that modify that data while
keeping it safe via encapsulation.
concept_explanation.when_to_use:
Whenever you transition
from learning syntax to building actual tools or applications.

breakdown_of_syntax.explanation:
The
'Manager' or 'Controller' class that orchestrates the entire application logic.

A dynamic collection used to
store multiple objects (like many books in a library) in memory.

Passing an object by
reference-to-const to avoid expensive copying while ensuring the original object
isn't modified.

rules:
Keep data members
private and use public methods for interaction.

Use descriptive names for
classes (e.g., 'Library' instead of 'LManager').

Always validate user input
(e.g., don't allow a negative bank deposit).

Separate display logic (cout)
from business logic (calculation) where possible.

Use modern C++ features
like range-based for loops for cleaner code.
key_points: OOP helps in mapping code to real-world objects.
Encapsulation protects the internal state of your project data.

Vectors are
the most common way to store multiple objects in a mini-project.

A 'Manager'
class helps centralize control of the system.

Code reusability is a primary
goal of modular project design.

Error handling ensures the program doesn't
crash on invalid user input.

Dry running logic is essential before writing
the code.

The 'this' pointer can be used to distinguish between member
variables and parameters.

Building a simple UI via the console is the first
step in project development.

code_examples.title:
Simple
Calculator (OOP Style)
code_examples.description:
A basic calculator demonstrating how to
wrap functionality inside a class for better organization.

code_examples.title:
Practical:
Bank Account System
code_examples.description:
A simplified banking system showing class
interaction, private data, and a vector of accounts.

In the Bank Account project, when we push BankAccount objects
into a vector, they are stored contiguously in memory on the Heap (since
std::vector manages dynamic memory). Each BankAccount object contains its own
'owner' string and 'balance' double.
visualization_steps:
Vector Header:
Points to memory address 0x100.
Each object is a distinct instance with its own memory footprint.

real_world_use_cases:
Banking Software: Managing millions of user
accounts with secure transactions.

E-commerce Inventory: Keeping track of
product stock, prices, and categories.

Student Information Systems: Handling
grades, attendance, and registration in universities.

Task Management Apps:
Storing and sorting user 'Todo' items.
common_mistakes:
Directly
accessing private members (e.g., acc.balance = 100) instead of using methods.
Forgetting to validate user input, leading to negative money or empty names.

Creating logic that relies on hard-coded array sizes instead of using
std::vector.

Writing all code in main() instead of splitting it into
classes.
best_practices:
Always mark getter methods as 'const' (e.g.,
double getBalance() const).

Use a switch statement for a clean console-based
menu system.

Initialize member variables using Constructor Initialization
Lists for better performance.

Pass large objects by constant reference to
avoid unnecessary memory overhead.

faq.question:
Why use
std::vector instead of a standard array for projects?
faq.answer:
A vector is
dynamic. You don't know how many books or accounts a user will add, so a vector
grows automatically as needed.

faq.question:
What is the purpose of a
'Manager' class?
faq.answer:
It serves as a controller that handles the logic
of adding, deleting, and searching through the data objects.

key_takeaways:
Mini projects bridge the gap between syntax and software
engineering.

Class interactions are the heart of professional C++
development.

Encapsulation ensures that data can only be modified through
controlled logic.

STL vectors are powerful tools for managing object
collections.
summary.preview_of_next_topic:
In Phase 16, we will look into
Advanced STL Containers and how to optimize our projects using Maps and Sets.

questions.question:
In a Library Management System, why should the 'bookStatus'
(Available/Borrowed) be private?
questions.answer:
To prevent external code from
changing the status without following proper logic (like checking if the user is
authorized), ensuring data integrity.

questions.question:
What is the benefit of having a separate class
for 'Transaction' in a Bank System rather than just a function?
questions.answer:
A
Transaction class can store history (date, time, amount, type) for every action,
allowing for audit logs and undo operations, which is better for organization
and scalability.

questions.question:
Write a method for a 'Book' class that checks if a specific
title matches the book's title (case-sensitive).

How would you implement a search functionality in a 'Library' class that
returns a pointer to a 'Book' object from a vector of books if found?
questions.answer:
Book* findBook(std::string title) { for(auto& b : books) {
if(b.getTitle() == title) return &b; } return nullptr; }

It fails to check if 'amt' is positive and if 'balance' is
greater than or equal to 'amt', potentially leading to negative balances.

questions.question:
If you
store pointers in a vector (std::vector<Book*>) in your project, what critical
step must you perform in the destructor of the Manager class?
questions.answer:
You
must iterate through the vector and manually 'delete' every pointer to prevent
memory leaks, as the vector only deletes the pointer address, not the allocated
memory.

questions.question:
Complete the logic for a Todo App to mark a task as completed based on its
index.
questions.answer:
void markDone(int index) { if(index >= 0 && index <
tasks.size()) { tasks[index].isCompleted = true; } }

questions.question:
Create a function signature
and logic for 'Transfer' between two BankAccount objects.

mcqs.question:
Which OOP principle
is primarily used when we group attributes like 'title' and 'author' into a
'Book' class?

In a menu-driven program, which structure is best for
handling different user choices (1, 2, 3...)?

What happens if you use
'std::vector::push_back()' with a class that has no default constructor but has
a parameterized one?

options.B:
Compiler
error unless you provide arguments
options.D:
The
vector fills with null values

mcqs.question:
Given: std::vector v = {10, 20}; v.push_back(30); cout
<< v.size(); What is the output?

If a class member is 'const double pi = 3.14;', can a method in the
class change its value?

options.A: Yes, if the method is public
options.B: Yes, if the method is not const

mcqs.C: Everything is correct (string converts to Todo via constructor)

code_examples.question:
What is wrong here: 'void addGrade(int g) {
grades.push_back(g); }' if grades is defined as 'int grades[10];'?

options.A: push_back is a vector method, not for arrays
options.B:
The grade must be a
float

code_examples.question:
To allow a BankAccount to start with zero balance if not specified:
'BankAccount(double b = ____) : balance(b) {}'

What is the best way to handle a 'Delete User' operation
in a project with a vector?

options.A: Set the user object to null
